title: Using grey water for garden irrigation
subtitle: Bath and shower water for established trees and shrubs, not vegetable beds
excerpt: Greywater from baths and showers can be bucketed or piped directly onto garden soil. It contains small amounts of soap, skin cells, and hair, none of which harm established trees, shrubs, or lawns. It should not be used on vegetable beds, seedlings, or plants with edible above-ground parts due to soap residues and potential bacterial content.
sourceNotes: Environment Agency greywater reuse guidance (2011). Waterwise greywater for gardens advice. WRAP water efficiency guidance for gardens.

content.text: used directly on garden soil, without storage, is the lowest-risk approach to reuse. A bath uses 150-180 litres; a 10-minute shower 100-150 litres. Applied immediately after use, the bacterial content is low and the soap concentration, while detectable, does not harm established woody plants. The Environment Agency endorses direct-to-garden use for private dwellings.
Established trees and shrubs, safe; roots are deep and not in contact with surface water.
Lawns, safe, but prolonged use of hard water mixed with soap can raise soil pH over years. Alternate with mains water.
Fruit trees and bushes, safe, applied to soil only, not leaves or fruit.
Salad crops, leaf vegetables, or anything eaten raw. The water can pass bacteria onto the leaves.
Seedlings or young plants, soap residues can damage fine roots and tender growth.
Edible crops where water contacts the edible part directly.
Any stored greywater more than 24 hours old, bacterial content increases rapidly with time and temperature.
Step 1, place a bucket in the shower.
A 10-15 litre bucket catches the run-off while showering. One shower fills it once or twice. Empty it directly onto tree or shrub roots within a few hours. Do not store overnight.
For baths: use a bucket or a hand pump to transfer water from the bath to the garden via a window. A pump and hose system allows draining the bath without lifting heavy buckets.

Standard shower gel, shampoo, and soap are tolerated well by established plants. Avoid using water from baths where products containing high concentrations of bleach, disinfectant, or strong detergent have been used.
